questions.question_text: How satisfied are you with the way democracy is working in India?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat satisfied. Democracy in India is functioning — elections happen, governments change, courts still operate — but there are real concerns I cannot ignore about press freedom, economic disparity, and social harmony. I'm neither dismissive of what's working nor blind to what isn't.

Somewhat satisfied — B. Democracy gives us a voice and there are some programs that help, but it's not working as well as it should for people in my situation. I'm pragmatic about it — I don't think any other system would necessarily be better, but I can't pretend everything is fine either.

Somewhat satisfied — B. Democracy in India is functioning in the sense that elections are held, voices are heard to some degree, but the lived reality for people like me — financial strain, healthcare anxiety, systemic gaps — means I cannot say I am very satisfied. There is progress but also deep frustration.

C — Not too satisfied. I believe in democracy as an idea and I value my vote, but the reality I live — struggling financially, watching flood damage go unaddressed properly, feeling like government schemes don't always reach people like me — makes me feel the system isn't working as well as it should for ordinary people.

B — Somewhat satisfied. Democracy in India has real strengths: free elections, a free press to some degree, peaceful transfers of power. But corruption, economic inequality, and the gap between policy and implementation leave me with reservations. It's working, but not as well as it could.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of Prime Minister Narendra Modi?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat favorable. I see genuine accomplishments in governance and infrastructure, and I appreciate decisive leadership. But I hold real concerns about social harmony and some economic outcomes, so I can't say 'very favorable.' As a pragmatic moderate, somewhat favorable reflects my honest, nuanced assessment.

Somewhat favorable. I acknowledge some genuine achievements — welfare schemes, infrastructure, India's global standing — but I hold real reservations about inflation, the impact on small traders like my husband's shop, and economic hardship for families like mine. I can't give 'very favorable' when my kitchen budget tells a different story every month.

B — Somewhat favorable. I can acknowledge some genuine achievements — particularly welfare schemes that could help people like me — while maintaining my moderate, pragmatic skepticism. I'm not a devoted supporter but I'm not hostile either. The healthcare schemes give me some reassurance. The inflation and economic pressures on my household temper my enthusiasm.

I would say somewhat favorable. I respect the energy and some welfare programs that have reached villages, but I have real concerns about economic hardship continuing for people like me. I am pragmatic — I don't give full credit or full blame to one person. Somewhat favorable reflects my balanced, moderate view.

B — Somewhat favorable. He has shown strong leadership on certain infrastructure and national presence fronts, and I appreciate decisiveness in governance. But I have real reservations about social polarization and economic inequality. 'Somewhat favorable' reflects my honest, nuanced position.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP)?
sample_responses: Somewhat unfavorable — B is too positive given my minority community concerns, but I'm not 'very unfavorable' either because I acknowledge some governance achievements and I'm a moderate who doesn't take extreme positions. I'll go with somewhat unfavorable.

Somewhat unfavorable — leaning this way because of the economic hardship I've personally experienced, the struggles for small traders and families like mine. I appreciate some welfare initiatives but the overall cost of living pressure and lack of sufficient support for people in our situation makes me lean toward unfavorable, though not strongly so.

B — Somewhat favorable. I appreciate the national vision and some governance improvements, but I have real reservations about economic management and how policies affect people like me at the ground level. I'm not a partisan supporter — this is a measured, qualified view.

Somewhat unfavorable. I'm not strongly against BJP but I feel they haven't delivered enough for people in my situation. The big national rhetoric doesn't match what I see locally. I'm a moderate — I give credit where it's due — but on balance, my lived experience tips me toward somewhat unfavorable.

B — Somewhat favorable. I give credit where it is due — some welfare schemes, infrastructure, stability — but I have real reservations about economic pressures on ordinary families, farm policy, and divisive moments. I am not fully on board, but not opposed either.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Indian National Congress (INC / Congress party)?
sample_responses: Somewhat unfavorable — not strongly, but I've seen enough governance failures, corruption issues, and weak leadership from Congress in recent years that I lean negative. I respect their historical role but present performance matters more to me.

Somewhat unfavorable — not strongly against them, but they haven't given me enough concrete reason to feel positive. The dynastic leadership, corruption associations, and their current weak state make me skeptical. But I won't say very unfavorable because I'm moderate and pragmatic — they've done some good things historically.

C — Somewhat unfavorable. I'm not a BJP loyalist, but Congress in its current form feels weakened, lacking strong leadership, and too focused on dynasty politics. Their economic management had some positives but also serious failures. As a moderate, I can't say very unfavorable, but I lean somewhat unfavorable based on their recent performance and disconnect from people like me.

Somewhat unfavorable. I don't hate Congress, but they've been in power for long stretches and haven't fundamentally improved conditions for people like me. The dynasty politics bothers me as a pragmatist. I can see some value in their welfare history, but the corruption and disconnect from real struggles tips me toward unfavorable — though not strongly so.

C — Somewhat unfavorable. As a pragmatic moderate, I recognize Congress's historical contributions but feel their recent leadership, strategic direction, and inability to present a compelling alternative narrative leaves me more skeptical than supportive. They haven't earned a favorable view recently through tangible outcomes.

questions.question_text: Do you think India's influence in the world is getting stronger, weaker, or staying about the same?
sample_responses: A — Getting stronger. As a moderate, I can acknowledge that India's global footprint genuinely has grown — economically, diplomatically, in terms of soft power and diaspora influence. I have reservations about some domestic trends, but on balance, India's international influence has risen in recent years.

C — Staying about the same. I think India has some genuine achievements internationally — space, diplomacy, G20 hosting — but the internal struggles, economic pressures on ordinary people like me, and the mixed signals from different news sources make me feel it's not dramatically stronger. I'm a moderate, I see both sides. Net-net: roughly the same, maybe slightly improving in some areas, declining in others.

C — Staying about the same. India has made some gains but also faces real headwinds. As a moderate who looks at evidence case by case, I can't confidently say 'getting stronger' across the board. Some areas yes, but overall the picture is mixed — which to me means roughly the same.

C — Staying about the same. I can see some positive developments internationally, but I also see real problems at home that make me doubt the strength narrative. As a pragmatic moderate, I think the honest answer is that it's roughly the same, with some improvements in some areas and weaknesses in others.

A — Getting stronger. On balance, India's global footprint has genuinely expanded in recent years. The economic trajectory, strategic partnerships, space achievements, and diplomatic positioning give me enough reason to lean this way — even if I hold reservations about internal challenges limiting full potential.

questions.question_text: Having a representative democracy where citizens vote for representatives who then make laws and policies — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
sample_responses: Somewhat good. I believe representative democracy is genuinely positive for India, but I'm realistic — it has real weaknesses like corruption, vote-bank politics, and slow decision-making. I won't say 'very good' because I've seen how the system can be manipulated and how ordinary people sometimes feel unheard. But overall, it's the right framework for a diverse country like India.

Somewhat good. Representative democracy is genuinely good for India — it gives citizens voice and accountability. But I can't say 'very good' because I've seen how corruption, money power, and caste politics distort it. The system works better in theory than in practice sometimes. Still, it's far better than having no elections at all.

B — Somewhat good. I believe in representative democracy for India, but I can't say 'very good' because I see real problems — representatives who don't truly serve ordinary people, corruption, vote-bank politics. It works, it's the right system for India's diversity, but it has serious flaws that hold it back from being truly excellent.

I would say somewhat good. Representative democracy is a good system in principle — people choosing their own representatives is important and gives accountability. But I have seen how money, caste, and corruption can distort who actually gets represented. So it is good but not perfect — somewhat good.

B — Somewhat good. Representative democracy is genuinely good for India, but I cannot say 'very good' because I have seen how money, caste, and corruption distort the system. In theory it is excellent; in practice it is imperfect. So somewhat good — good in principle, with real-world limitations.

questions.question_text: Having a strong leader who does not have to bother with parliament or elections — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
sample_responses: C — Somewhat bad. I can appreciate strong decisive leadership, but completely bypassing parliament and elections goes too far. Those institutions, imperfect as they are, provide accountability. I'd say somewhat bad rather than very bad because I understand the appeal of cutting through gridlock — but the risks outweigh the benefits.

C — Somewhat bad. I'm a pragmatic person. Strong leadership is good, but completely bypassing parliament and elections removes the one protection ordinary citizens have. That's not good governance, that's just power with no leash.

C — Somewhat bad. I'm a moderate, not an ideologue. I can appreciate strong leadership, but removing parliament AND elections together is too much. That's not just decisive governance — that's removing the safeguards entirely. Somewhat bad, not very bad, because I acknowledge the appeal of efficiency, but the risks outweigh the benefits.

Somewhat bad. I understand the appeal of strong leadership — I want someone decisive who can actually fix our roads, our water supply, our schools. But bypassing parliament and elections removes the only protection ordinary people like me have. Without elections, we become invisible completely.

C — Somewhat bad. I can see the appeal of decisive leadership, but removing parliament and elections entirely takes away the checks that protect us when things go wrong. A strong leader within democratic structures — that I support. But no accountability at all? That worries me.

questions.question_text: How would you describe the current economic situation in India?
sample_responses: C — Somewhat bad. I'm not saying it's catastrophic, and I recognize there are pockets of growth and progress. But for the average middle-class or lower-income family, the economic situation feels more difficult than comfortable right now. Inflation, unemployment concerns, and cost-of-living pressures are real.

C — Somewhat bad. It's not completely collapsed, there are some sectors doing well, some people doing fine. But for middle-lower income households like mine, the economic situation is genuinely difficult. I wouldn't say 'very bad' because I can see some infrastructure improvement and some opportunities exist for others. But 'somewhat good' would be dishonest given what I live every day.

C — Somewhat bad. I'm a moderate, not an extreme voice. I won't say 'very bad' because I do see some progress in infrastructure and opportunities in certain sectors. But from where I stand — struggling with monthly bills, watching my savings being eaten by inflation — the economy is not serving ordinary people like me well enough. Somewhat bad is honest and fair.

C — Somewhat bad. I won't say 'very bad' because I can see some things functioning — roads, some government schemes, mobile connectivity improved. But for ordinary people like me, daily life feels harder economically. I'm pragmatic — I won't be extreme in either direction — but the honest answer is somewhat bad.

C — Somewhat bad. I'm not saying the economy is collapsing, there are some positive developments, but for ordinary households like mine, the cost of living pressure is real and significant. I take a case-by-case, pragmatic view rather than a partisan one.

questions.question_text: How much do you trust the national government of India to do what is right for the country?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat. As a moderate, I see genuine efforts in some domains but real failures and concerns in others. I'm not a BJP loyalist who trusts completely, nor am I a strong opposition voice who distrusts entirely. 'Somewhat' honestly captures my nuanced, case-by-case assessment.
Somewhat — B. As a moderate, I take a case-by-case view. The government does some things right but also fails in many areas. I trust them somewhat, not a lot, not dismissively.

B — Somewhat. I trust the national government to do what is right sometimes, but not always. There are genuine achievements but also failures and broken promises. I'm pragmatic, not ideological about this.

B — Somewhat. As a pragmatic moderate, I can't say 'a lot' — I haven't seen enough direct benefit in my own life. But I also won't say 'not much' or 'not at all' because some things have improved and I try to judge case by case. 'Somewhat' reflects my honest, nuanced position.

B — Somewhat. The government does some things right, particularly on infrastructure and certain welfare schemes, but there are real gaps in healthcare access and accountability that I've seen firsthand. My trust is conditional and partial.

questions.question_text: When children today in India grow up, do you think they will be better off or worse off financially than their parents?
sample_responses: A — Better off. Despite real challenges like cost of living and inequality, the overall direction of opportunity in India is upward. I'm cautiously optimistic.

B — Worse off. Not because I've lost hope, but because I'm being honest. Prices keep rising, jobs are uncertain, climate is affecting farmers and small businesses. My moderate, pragmatic side tells me the structural challenges are real and growing. I worry my children will face even harder financial pressures than we did, despite having more education and opportunity on paper.

I'll say Better off — but with real hesitation. India is growing, there are more opportunities than when my parents were young, digital economy is opening doors. But I worry inequality is widening and those without connections or capital will struggle. On balance, I think the next generation has more tools available, even if the challenges are also bigger. Cautious optimism.

B — Worse off. I want to believe in a better future, and I pray for it, but looking at rising costs, climate problems, job uncertainty, and what people like us face every day, I think children today will have a harder financial road than we did.

A — Better off. India is on an upward economic trajectory overall. Education access has improved, digital opportunities are expanding, and the aspiration and work ethic of the younger generation gives me hope. Yes, challenges exist — inequality, cost of living, climate pressures — but on balance, I believe children today will be financially better off than their parents.

questions.question_text: How important is religion in your life?
sample_responses: A — Very important. Religion is not just important; it is foundational to how I live, make decisions, find meaning, and connect with others.
A — Very important. Religion is central to my daily life, my values, how I raise my children, how I cope with hardship. There is no other honest answer.
A — Very important. Religion and faith are central to who I am, how I make decisions, how I cope with hardship, and how I connect with my family and community.

A — Very important. There is no hesitation here. Religion is not a hobby or a background preference for me. It is the foundation I stand on, especially when everything else is uncertain or falling apart.
A — Very important. There is no hesitation here. Religion isn't just a background element; it is the framework through which I interpret everything else.

questions.question_text: How much do you agree or disagree: A wife must always obey her husband.
sample_responses: C — Somewhat disagree. I believe in mutual respect and partnership in marriage. A wife should respect her husband, yes, but 'always obey' is too absolute and removes her own judgment and dignity. I hold a nuanced view here.

Somewhat disagree. Marriage requires respect and cooperation. A wife should consider her husband's views and in many cases defer, but 'always obey' is too absolute. There are times when a wife's judgment, wisdom, or protection of the family requires her to speak up or act independently. I learned this the hard way.

B — Somewhat agree. I believe in traditional family structure and the husband's role as head of the family, which is consistent with my faith and upbringing. But 'always' and 'must' is too rigid. A good marriage involves mutual respect and consultation, even within traditional roles.

B — Somewhat agree. I believe in respect, deference in many matters, and harmony in marriage. There is wisdom in a wife considering her husband's guidance seriously. But 'always obey' goes too far for me. I choose somewhat agree as a nod to the cultural and spiritual value of marital respect, while acknowledging that blind obedience is not something I can fully endorse given my own life experience.

C — Somewhat disagree. Marriage involves respect, cooperation, and sometimes deference — but not unconditional obedience. A wife should listen to and consider her husband's views, and he should do the same. 'Always obey' removes her agency entirely, which goes against both my faith and my lived experience.

questions.question_text: When jobs are scarce, men should have more right to a job than women. Do you agree or disagree?
sample_responses: C — Somewhat disagree. I'm not going all the way to 'completely disagree' because I do acknowledge that in Indian society there are complex realities around family responsibilities and traditional expectations. But fundamentally, I believe women have equal right to employment, especially since many women are supporting families just as men are. So I somewhat disagree rather than completely, acknowledging the complexity without endorsing the premise.

Somewhat disagree. I understand why some people feel this way — the idea that men must provide — but my own life has shown me that this thinking leaves families vulnerable. Women who need work have just as much right to it. I stop short of 'completely disagree' only because I hold nuanced views and understand the cultural context, but my experience tilts me firmly toward disagreement.

I somewhat disagree. I can understand the traditional sentiment behind this view, and I won't completely dismiss the cultural logic, but as a pragmatist I think denying women jobs simply because of gender is wasteful and unfair — especially when women's income can be just as vital to family survival as men's.

I somewhat disagree. I have seen that when jobs are scarce, families need everyone who can work to work. Denying women that right hurts households like mine. But I am not completely against the traditional view — I understand where it comes from, and I hold a nuanced position.

C — Somewhat disagree. I lean toward disagreement but I hold a nuanced view. In practice I know some families rely entirely on male income and there are social realities. But as a principle, I cannot say men should have more right to a job simply because of gender. I would somewhat disagree rather than completely disagree because I acknowledge the complexity of traditional family structures without endorsing blanket discrimination.

questions.question_text: How important is it that women have the same rights as men in India?
sample_responses: A — Very important. Women having the same rights as men is something I believe in deeply, both from my faith perspective and my lived experience as a woman navigating life in India.

A — Very important. I've lived the consequences of women not having full rights. Economic rights, legal rights, property rights — these are life and death matters for families like mine, not just ideals.

A — Very important. Women having the same rights as men is very important. Equal rights in education, law, economic opportunity — these strengthen families and society. I hold some traditional views on family roles, but that's different from denying rights. Rights are foundational.

A — Very important. Women having equal rights is not just an abstract political idea to me. It is about survival, dignity, and opportunity. I have seen what happens when women are not protected or respected. Equal rights are fundamental.

A — Very important. As a woman, as a Sikh, as someone who has watched what happens when women lack equal standing, I believe this strongly. Equal rights for women isn't a Western import — it's a matter of justice and practical benefit for all of Indian society.

questions.question_text: How much of a threat is global climate change to India?
sample_responses: A — Major threat. India is highly vulnerable to climate impacts: monsoon disruption, coastal flooding, extreme heat, water scarcity. I've observed some of these effects locally. As a moderate who takes evidence seriously and cross-references sources, I can't downplay this. Economic development matters, but not at the cost of ignoring existential environmental risks.
A

A — Major threat. The combination of what I observe locally, what credible experts say, and the real economic vulnerability of people like me makes this clearly a major threat. I hold nuanced views, but on this specific question, the evidence points strongly in one direction.

A — Major threat. My lived experience leaves no room for doubt. That flood wasn't a freak event — things are changing, and the poorest communities like mine are hit hardest. We don't have the savings or the resources to rebuild again and again. Climate change is not a future problem for me. It already happened.

A — Major threat. India is especially vulnerable — coastal areas, agriculture, water resources, heat stress on poor populations. I can hold this view while also believing economic development matters. These are not mutually exclusive. The evidence from lived experience and credible reporting points to this being a serious, major threat.
